(collection ETable exported to file incomeeducationreg.docx)

. 


The table above produces three linear regression models using the Chan-Goldthorpe scale, my social status measure, and the Cambridge scale as dependent variables against median income and proportion of A’levels measures outlined above. The rankings derived from both my own measure and the Chan-Goldthorpe measure produce near identical results – there is some substantive difference in the Cambridge scale however. In this rudimentary regression model, the conclusions found from Chan and Goldthorpe (2004) hold for my measure as well. If these findings are duplicated across multiple models then this would provide strong evidence against the use of my measure. The conclusion would be that the addition of other Weberian measures to social status does not capture a more definitive variance in the form of social status. If however differences emerge, there is a strong case to use my measure over the Chan-Goldthorpe scale, as variance is being captured through the addition of other Weberian concepts added to my measure that is absent in their own. Chan and Goldthorpe do however provide much of the groundwork and evidence to suggest that a measure based on this Weberian construction is sufficiently different from Duncan’s SEI and Ganzeboom and Treiman’s occupational status scale (Blau and Duncan, 1967; Ganzeboom and Treiman, 2003; Chan and Goldthorpe, 2004).

Test Regressions
The case that this measure is sociologically more compelling has been made. Hitherto, there has yet to be strong evidence to suggest that my measure is in fact more effective in capturing Weberian social status than the Chan-Goldthorpe measure. The next step is to provided evidence to suggest that this measure provides substantively different results from the Chan-Goldthorpe measure. If that holds true then their argument that occupations and occupations alone are all that is required to measure social status holds, and the need for this new measure is not needed. If that does not hold, then the latent features underpinning this new measure of social status appear to be capturing some element of status that the original Chan-Goldthorpe measure fails to account for. This paper first conducts a variety of models based on key assumptions underpinning Weberian social status. Models related to cultural consumption, social behaviour and social attitudes are conducted to assess the similarities or differences present between my social status measure and the Chan-Goldthorpe measure. The Cambridge scale is included as a vital comparison measure. A model on life chances is also included to provide a suitable test of my social status measure – social status should have no impact on economic life chances as Weber defines this as a social class-based concept. These first set of models are simplistic models that only include measures of social class, and the given status measure. For non-linear models, relevant KHB analysis is taken place to assess rescaling as a potential limiting factor in comparing nested non-linear models.
After these models are interpreted, this paper then turns to the British Social Attitudes Survey of 2002 to duplicate prior analysis by Chan and Goldthorpe (2007) looking at left-right and libertarian-authoritarian axis. I attempt to duplicate their analysis, then compare with my own social status measure to assess differences.
Logistic Regression of Smoking Status.

%%stata

collect clear 

gen smoke=.
replace smoke=0 if bj_smoker==1
replace smoke=1 if bj_smoker==2

svy: logit smoke ib3.nssec
etable 

svy: logit smoke factor1_z ib3.nssec
etable, append
khb logit smoke ib3.nssec || factor1_z , summary

svy: logit smoke chanstat_z ib3.nssec
etable, append
khb logit smoke ib3.nssec || chanstat_z , summary


svy: logit smoke cambridge_z ib3.nssec
etable, append
khb logit smoke ib3.nssec || cambridge_z , summary

collect style showbase all

collect label levels etable_depvar 1 "Null Model + NS-SEC" ///
                                        2 "Social Status" /// 
                                        3 "Chan-Goldthorpe" ///
                                        4 "Cambridge Scale" , modify

collect style cell, font(Book Antiqua)

etable, replay column(depvar) ///
cstat(_r_b, nformat(%4.2f))  ///
        cstat(_r_se, nformat(%6.2f))  ///
        showstars showstarsnote  ///
        stars(.05 "*" .01 "**" .001 "***", attach(_r_b)) ///
        mstat(N) mstat(aic) mstat(bic) mstat(r2_a)  ///
        title("Table 1: Regression Models of Smoking Status") ///
        titlestyles(font(Arial Narrow, size(14) bold)) ///
        note("Data Source: BHPS. Adjusted for Complex Sample. N=6,959") ///
        notestyles(font(Book Antiqua, size(8) italic)) ///
        export("smokingstatus.docx", replace)


.

. 


The first model presented in teh table above is a logistic regression model looking at a specific type of consumption practice – smoking. The dependent variable smoking status [bj_smoker] is a binary indicator of if an individual has never smoked or not. The initial null model with just social class presents a clear story, there is a class-based effect of class membership and smoking status. For those blue-collar workers, they have a lower log odds to never have smoked compared to NS-SEC 2. This class effect almost entirely disappears when my social status measure is included in the model. Only NS-SEC 1.2 and NS-SEC 7, the extremes of social class schema remain significant. The substantive effect transfers from a class-based effect to a status-based effect. As social status increases the log likelihood that an individual has never smoked also increases. This ‘switch’ from a class to status-based effect does not occur when using the Chan-Goldthorpe measure. Only NS-SEC 4 is insignificant in the Chan-Goldthorpe model compared to the null model. According to the Chan-Goldthorpe model smoking status is influenced by social status and social class. This theoretically goes against the core arguments of Weberian distinctions between social status and social class. Cultural consumption patterns are determined by status in the Weberian tradition. The Cambridge scale model presents a near identical interpretation to my own social status model.
Voting Status.

. 


The next model is another logistic regression, this time purporting to measure social behaviour. Another binary dependent variable, this time a variable of voting status [bj_vote7] indicating if a person has voted in the last general election or not. A similar pattern emerges. The initial null model with only social class presents a clear social class effect on voting status, with those from blue collar occupations having a higher log likelihood of not voting compared to NS-SEC 2. This class effect all but disappears with the exception of NS-SEC 3 and NS-SEC 7 when my social status measure is introduced. As social status increases, there is a decreased log likelihood for individuals to have not voted in the most recent election. Whilst my own social status measure and the Chan-Goldthorpe measure produce similar point estimates, the social class effect once more remains for the Chan-Goldthorpe model compared to my own social status model. These models suggest that voting apathy or behaviour is a matter of social status over social class. That is captured within my social status measure but not the Chan-Goldthorpe model, even though their point estimates remain similar.
Attitudes

(collection ETable exported to file attitudereg.docx)

. 


The next set of models focuses on forms of social attitudes. These models focus on a particular attitude question asked of BHPS members. A Likert scale is constructed based on the response to the prompt that it is ‘unfair that wealth buys medical priority’ [bj_ophlc]. Ordinal logistic regressions are produced for each social status measure. The null model with NS-SEC suggests a modest class-based effect present. Particularly those at the most privileged end of NS-SEC have a higher log odds of strongly disagreeing to this statement compared to NS-SEC 2. The addition of my social status measure actually produces a stronger social class effect across the NS-SEC schema, making all but one category of NS-SEC (4) significant. Those in blue collar occupations as well as NS-SEC 3 all have a decreased log likelihood of strongly agreeing to this statement compared to NS-SEC 2. In addition to the stronger class effect, there is also a social status impact. As social status increases there is a decreased log likelihood of strongly disagreeing to this statement. What is interesting in this model is that it clearly demonstrates the differences between social class and social status. For those at the very top of the status scale, they are more likely to strongly disagree with such a statement, whereas those at the top of the class schema are most likely to disagree with such a statement. A similar effect is reported using the Chan-Goldthorpe measure, though does not capture NS-SEC 7. The Cambridge scale does not report a significant finding.
Economic Life Chances

(collection ETable exported to file unemploymentspell.docx)

. 


Economic life chances should not have any relationship to social status according to Weber. These next set of models test this assumption against all three scales. These models use a logistic regression model with a dependent variable of unemployment measuring if an individual in the past year has undergone a single spell of unemployment or not. The null model with social class presents a clear social class based effect. The social class effect is not disrupted by the inclusion of either my own social status variable or the Chan-Goldthorpe scale but is by the Cambridge scale. Importantly all three scales do not report significance. My measure can adequately be argued to be an effective measure of social status that does not have an influence on economic life chances.
Homosexual Preference

collect clear 

gen homo=.
replace homo= bj_opfamr if bj_opfamr>=1 & bj_opfamr<=5

etable

etable, append 
margins, at(factor1_z=(-2.0(0.1)2.0))
marginsplot, recast(line) 

svy: ologit homo chanstat_z ib3.nssec
etable, append 
margins, at(chanstat_z=(-1.5(0.1)1.2))
marginsplot, recast(line) 

svy: ologit homo cambridge_z ib3.nssec

margins, at(cambridge_z=(-2.0(0.1)3.0))

Finally, the next set of models uses an ordinal logistic regression model on a Likert scale of views towards homosexuality [bj_opfamr]. This is the last set of models using the BHPS data. The initial null model plus social class appears to present a clear social class effect at the blue-collar level. Blue collar occupations have a decreased log likelihood strongly disagreeing to the view that homosexual relationships are wrong. The introduction of my social status measure nearly wipes away the class effect and suggests that most all of the influence comes from social status rather than social class. As social status increases there is an increased likelihood to strongly disagree with the statement that homosexual relationships are wrong. Identical findings are reported in the Chan-Goldthorpe model.

title: Weberian Social Status Reimagined: A Sociological and Empirical Critique of Existing Status Measures and a Viable Alternative
section: Duplication of Chan and Goldthorpe (2007) British Social Attitudes Survey 2001
text:
Duplication of Chan and Goldthorpe (2007) British Social Attitudes Survey 2001
All models produced so far have been simplistic constructions that solely use NS-SEC and social status. These next set of models attempt to produce a ‘real life’ model example that would be published in academic literature. These models seek to duplicate analysis from Chan and Goldthorpe (2007). They create models based on the determinants of political attitudes on left-right and libertarian-authoritarian scales using linear regression. These models use the 2002 British Social Attitudes Survey. By using the useful appendix of their article, the models have been reconstructed. The sole difference is that my analysis uses the 2001 British Social Attitudes Survey for one important reason. The original analysis produced via Chan and Goldthorpe (2004), and my own analysis uses the BHPS wave 10 dataset that uses SOC 90 codes. The 2001 British Social Attitudes survey uses SOC 90 codes, where the 2002 survey uses SOC 2000 codes. This should require a re-analysis of a social status measure using the new SOC codes. For consistency, I continue to use the SOC 90 codes and so duplicate their work in the 2001 survey.
The dependent variable left-right was constructed through a variety of measures consisting of Likert scales on statements that the government should redistribute wealth [redistrb], big business benefits owners [bigbusnn], workers don’t get a fair share of wealth [wealth], one law for rich and another for poor [richlaw], and management will always try to get the better of employees if it gets the chance [indust4]. Independent variables consist of age [rage] between the ages 18-97, sex [rsex], household income [hhincome], highest educational qualification [hedqual], current NS-SEC [rnssec], and each model contains either my social status measure or the Chan-Goldthorpe scale, once more z standardised for comparison purposes.
The second dependent variable libertarian-authoritarian was constructed through similar Likert variables on statements that young people today don’t have enough respect for traditional British values [tradvals], people who break the law should be given stiffer sentences [stifsent], for some crimes, the death penalty is appropriate [deathapp], schools should teach children to obey [obey], the law should always be obeyed [wronglaw], censorship of films and magazines is necessary to uphold moral standards [censor]. This model also includes all independent variables mentioned above.
The left-right models are representative of economic life chances and so both social status measures should not unduly influence the model. Both models present identical results. Neither social status measure is statistically significant. This presents evidence that both measures adequately capture the Weberian notion that social status has little to no influence on individual economic life chances. The second set of models assessing libertarian-authoritarian axis has no social class-based effect and both measures of social status are statistically significant. There is however a large difference between the two models. My social status measure has a point estimate that is 55 percent larger than the Chan-Goldthorpe scale.

gen redis=.
replace redis=1 if redistrb==1
replace redis=2 if redistrb==2
replace redis=3 if redistrb==3
replace redis=4 if redistrb==4
replace redis=5 if redistrb==5

gen big=.
replace big=1 if bigbusnn==1
replace big=2 if bigbusnn==2
replace big=3 if bigbusnn==3
replace big=4 if bigbusnn==4
replace big=5 if bigbusnn==5

gen fair=. 
replace fair=1 if wealth==1
replace fair=2 if wealth==2
replace fair=3 if wealth==3
replace fair=4 if wealth==4
replace fair=5 if wealth==5

gen law=.
replace law=1 if richlaw==1
replace law=2 if richlaw==2
replace law=3 if richlaw==3
replace law=4 if richlaw==4
replace law=5 if richlaw==5

gen better=.
replace better=1 if indust4==1
replace better=2 if indust4==2
replace better=3 if indust4==3
replace better=4 if indust4==4
replace better=5 if indust4==5

gen leftright= redis+big+fair+law+better


gen trad=.
replace trad=1 if tradvals==1
replace trad=2 if tradvals==2
replace trad=3 if tradvals==3
replace trad=4 if tradvals==4
replace trad=5 if tradvals==5

gen stiff=.
replace stiff=1 if stifsent==1
replace stiff=2 if stifsent==2
replace stiff=3 if stifsent==3
replace stiff=4 if stifsent==4
replace stiff=5 if stifsent==5

gen death=.
replace death=1 if deathapp==1
replace death=2 if deathapp==2
replace death=3 if deathapp==3
replace death=4 if deathapp==4
replace death=5 if deathapp==5

gen school=.
replace school=1 if obey==1
replace school=2 if obey==2
replace school=3 if obey==3
replace school=4 if obey==4
replace school=5 if obey==5

gen wrong=.
replace wrong=1 if wronglaw==1
replace wrong=2 if wronglaw==2
replace wrong=3 if wronglaw==3
replace wrong=4 if wronglaw==4
replace wrong=5 if wronglaw==5

gen film=.
replace film=1 if censor==1
replace film=2 if censor==2
replace film=3 if censor==3
replace film=4 if censor==4
replace film=5 if censor==5

gen authlib= trad+stiff+death+school+wrong+film


gen age=. 
replace age= rage if rage>=18 & rage<=97

tab rsex

gen income=.
replace income=1 if hhincome>=1 & hhincome<=4
replace income=2 if hhincome>=5 & hhincome<=9
replace income=3 if hhincome>=10 & hhincome<=16
replace income=4 if hhincome>=17 

lab define income 1"<10k" 2"10-23k" 3"23-44k" 4">44k"
lab val income income

gen qual=. 
replace qual=1 if hedqual==7
replace qual=2 if hedqual==5
replace qual=3 if hedqual==4
replace qual=4 if hedqual==3
replace qual=5 if hedqual==2
replace qual=6 if hedqual==1

lab def qual 1"no qual" 2"CSE" 3"O'LEVEL" 4"A'LEVEL" 5"SUBDEGREE" 6"DEGREE"
lab val qual qual

gen nssec=.
replace nssec=1 if rnssec==2
replace nssec=2 if rnssec>=3.1 & rnssec<=3.4
replace nssec=3 if rnssec>=4 & rnssec<=6
replace nssec=4 if rnssec>=7 & rnssec<=7.4
replace nssec=5 if rnssec>=8 & rnssec<=9.2
replace nssec=6 if rnssec>=10 & rnssec<=11.2
replace nssec=7 if rnssec>=12 & rnssec<=12.7
replace nssec=8 if rnssec>=13 & rnssec<=13.5

lab def nssec 1"1.1" 2"1.2" 3"2" 4"3" 5"4" 6"5" 7"6" 8"7"
lab val nssec nssec


egen mean_status = mean(status)
egen sd_status = sd(status)
gen status_z = (status - mean_status) / sd_status

egen mean_chanstat= mean(chanstat)
egen sd_chanstat = sd(chanstat)
gen chanstat_z = (chanstat - mean_chanstat) / sd_chanstat

keep if !missing(leftright, age, rsex, income, qual, nssec, status_z, chanstat_z, authlib)

collect clear
regress leftright age rsex ib1.income ib1.qual ib3.nssec status_z
etable
regress leftright age rsex ib1.income ib1.qual ib3.nssec chanstat_z
etable, append

regress authlib age rsex ib1.income ib1.qual ib1.nssec c.status_z
etable, append

margins, at(status_z=(-1.60(0.12)2.10)) atmeans saving(plot1, replace)


marginsplot, ciopts(fcolor(blue%20)) ///
    title("Effect of Social Status on Libertarian-Authoritarian Axis") ///
    ytitle("Predicted Libertarian-Authoritarian Score") ///
    xtitle("Social Status") ///
    plotopts(lwidth(medium)) ///
    recast(line) ///
    xscale(range(-1.64 2.11)) ///
    name(plot1, replace) 




regress authlib age rsex ib1.income ib1.qual ib1.nssec c.chanstat_z
etable, append

margins, at(chanstat_z=(-1.62(0.10)1.60)) atmeans saving(plot2, replace)

marginsplot, ciopts(fcolor(blue%20)) ///
    title("Effect of Social Status on Libertarian-Authoritarian Axis") ///
    ytitle("Predicted Libertarian-Authoritarian Score") ///
    xtitle("Chan-Goldthorpe Scale") ///
    plotopts(lwidth(medium)) ///
    recast(line) ///
    xscale(range(-1.64 2.11)) ///
    name(plot2, replace) ///
    saving(plot2, replace)


graph combine plot1 plot2, ycommon


collect style showbase all

collect label levels etable_depvar 1 "Left-Right Social Status" ///
                                        2 "Left-Right Chan-Goldthorpe" /// 
                                        3 "Libertarian-Authoritarian Social Status" ///
                                        4 "Libertarian-Authoritarian Chan-Goldthorpe" , modify

collect style cell, font(Book Antiqua)

etable, replay column(depvar) ///
cstat(_r_b, nformat(%4.2f))  ///
        cstat(_r_se, nformat(%6.2f))  ///
        showstars showstarsnote  ///
        stars(.05 "*" .01 "**" .001 "***", attach(_r_b)) ///
        mstat(N) mstat(aic) mstat(bic) mstat(r2_a)  ///
        title("Table 1: Regression Models of Poltical Axis") ///
        titlestyles(font(Arial Narrow, size(14) bold)) ///
        note("Data Source: BHPS. Adjusted for Complex Sample. N=6,959") ///
        notestyles(font(Book Antiqua, size(8) italic)) ///
        export("polticalaxis.docx", replace)

. 























The size of this difference is better represented through marginal effects. The predicted probabilities at means are presented for each social status measure in each model. The probabilities are presented in figure XXXX. As can be seen the larger point estimate of my social status measure shows a much steeper gradient comparative to the Chan-Goldthorpe measure. The Chan-Goldthorpe measure presents the view that across the status scale there is a range of 13.48 to 14.21 on the authoritarian axis. For my social status measure there is a range of 13.17 to 14.69. My social status measure sees an extension on both extreme ends of the range of the authoritarian axis. Using my measure of social status, you would conclude that there is a larger influence of social status on the libertarian-authoritarian axis. Some may argue that this difference is relatively small, however this model amongst prior models have made it clear that my status measure appears to capture more ‘status’ through its addition of Weberian status concepts compared to the simple measure deployed by Chan-Goldthorpe.

title: Weberian Social Status Reimagined: A Sociological and Empirical Critique of Existing Status Measures and a Viable Alternative
text:
Conclusion
In this paper I have outlined current issues with Weberian constructions of social status as they exist in the Chan-Goldthorpe scale. This paper has outlined the theoretical issues with the partially Weberian operationalisation of social status and have provided an appropriate sociological justification for the inclusion of other aspects of Weberian social status to be included in any measure of status. This paper then follows a research programme adopting my theoretical recommendations into a newly operationalised measure of social status that is based in part on the original work coined by Chan and Goldthorpe (2004, 2007). Combining a MDSCAL method of homophily based on marriage occupational similarity with a factor analysis combining important aspects of Weberian social status: cultural consumption, friendship closeness, and monopolistic acquisition produces in this papers view an appropriate measure of social status that adequately captures the Weberian determinants of status that prior measures leave out. This construction is then directly compared with the Chan-Goldthorpe scale and the Cambridge scale to assess the relative similarity/difference in their use for statistical inference. This comparison is done in a variety of ways. Firstly, a basic assessment and comparison of my measure with education, income, and social class sees a relatively similar outcome to that of the Chan-Goldthorpe scale. On closer inspection using multiple different models assessing key elements of social status related to: cultural consumption, behaviours, attitudes, and life chances presents clear differences between my own measure and the Chan-Goldthorpe scale. These differences, in this papers view, are attributable to my social status measure being better capable of capturing the salient qualities of Weberian social status. In particular these differences appear in the form of wiping away social class-based effects compared to the Chan-Goldthorpe scale, and in certain models presenting a more sensitive overall measure of social status. In key areas both measures present identical results – in the case of life chances, but in areas of like attitudes, behaviours, and consumption, my status measure appears to be more sensitivity and salient to Weberian social status compared to the Chan-Goldthorpe scale.
There are several areas of limitations regarding the social status measure presented in this paper. The first concerns the lack of loading of emerging cultural consumption to the status measure. This may be a result of inappropriate or lack of sufficient emerging cultural consumption measures within the BHPS that thereby limits the loading used in the factor analysis. Recent analysis however appears to present an alternative view that emerging cultural consumption is no longer an effective proxy of social status as more and more elites consume emerging cultural aspects (Friedman and Reeves, 2024). Highbrow cultural consumption according to Friedman and Reeves (2024) continues to be a stratifying influence and also provides evidence as to why my highbrow measure loads well onto social status but the emerging measure does not.
The second limitation rests on the measures used to produce a friendship closeness variable. Weber speaks to closeness as a matter of ‘who you would sit down and eat dinner with’. No measure like this exists within the BHPS. The best alternative was a variety of measures related to how close an individual is with their best friends. This presented an interesting result whereby the less close individuals are with their best friends the higher social status that individual typically has. This specific role needs greater investigation.
The final weakness identified in this paper relates to social status only being constructed under a SOC 90 framework. For this status measure to be widely adopted, serval social status measures need to be constructed using multiple SOC versions. This is possible, though different surveys may need to be utilised to achieve this goal.

title: Weberian Social Status Reimagined: A Sociological and Empirical Critique of Existing Status Measures and a Viable Alternative
text:

title: Training
section: Handling Missing Data: A Practical Introduction To Techniques And Methods
text:
Handling Missing Data: A Practical Introduction To Techniques And Methods
Type: Workshop Delivered
Date: May 13th 2025 Location: Edinburgh Futures Institute, Edinburgh, Scotland
Description: This training event will cover teaching users about the Handling Missing Data techniques and how to deploy them into their own reserach agendas using popular data analyst software: Stata and R. Jupyter Notebooks, PowerPoint slide decks, and reference lists are provided. This training is split into four sessions, each increasing in speciality.
